1: Elihu continues. The speech now turns to the question of whether human behavior affects God — a question the Incarnation answers definitively: God is affected by human sin, not in the sense of being diminished, but in the sense that he chose to bear it (2 Cor Job 5:21).
2: “Do you think this is just when you say, ‘My righteousness is more than God’s’?” Elihu’s framing of Job’s complaint. The ultimate answer to the comparison between divine and human righteousness is Christ, who is both — divine righteousness incarnate (Rom Job 1:17) and the source of human righteousness by faith (Rom Job 3:22).
3: You say, “What does it benefit you? What do I gain from not sinning?” The cynical question that emptiness can produce. The resurrection answers it: “your labor in the Lord is not in vain” (1 Cor Job 15:58). The gain of righteousness is Christ himself — the surpassing worth of knowing him (Phil Job 3:8).
4: I will give you an answer, and your companions along with you. Elihu speaks to both Job and the friends. Christ speaks to the individual and to the community simultaneously: Heb Job 4:12 “the word of God is living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword,” piercing to the thoughts and intentions of every individual heart.
5: Look up at the sky and see; behold the clouds, higher than you. The call to lift the gaze heavenward. Heb Job 12:2: “looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith.” The upward look is to the ascended Christ who has gone higher than the clouds — “exalted above the heavens” (Heb Job 7:26).
6: If you sin, what do you accomplish against him? Elihu’s point about divine transcendence. But the Incarnation precisely reverses this: in Christ, God IS affected by human sin — Isa Job 53:4 “he has borne our griefs and carried our sorrows.” The transcendent God becomes the one who absorbs sin’s consequences.
7: If you are righteous, what do you give him? What does he receive from your hand? The question of what humans can offer God — which is nothing self-generated. 2 Cor Job 5:21: “for our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.” Christ gives the Father what humans cannot: perfect righteousness, on behalf of those who have none.
8: Your wickedness harms only someone like yourself; your righteousness benefits only other people. The horizontal consequences of sin and righteousness. But Christ’s righteousness has a vertical dimension: it accomplishes what no human righteousness could — the reconciliation of Creator and creature (Col Job 1:20).
9: Under the weight of great oppression people cry out; they call for help from the arm of the powerful. The universal human cry under oppression. Luke Job 4:18–19: “he has sent me to proclaim release to the captives... to set at liberty those who are oppressed, to proclaim the year of the Lord’s favor.” Christ is the arm of the powerful who responds to this cry.
10: “But no one asks, ‘Where is God my Maker, who gives songs in the night?’” The deeper question that the oppressed fail to ask. Acts Job 16:25: Paul and Silas in prison at midnight, singing hymns to God — the literal enactment of songs-in-the-night. Christ gives the capacity to sing in prison that Elihu identifies as the mark of true knowledge of God.
11: Who teaches us more than the animals of the earth and makes us wiser than the birds of the sky. The divine teaching that elevates humanity beyond the rest of creation. Matt Job 11:29: “take my yoke upon you and learn from me.” Col Job 2:3: “in him are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.” Christ is the teacher who makes the disciple wiser than all natural wisdom.
12: There they cry, but he does not answer, because of the pride of evil people. The unanswered cry of those who cry from wrong motives — pride rather than genuine need. Jas Job 4:6: “God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.” Christ’s answer is to those who humble themselves — “everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and everyone who humbles himself will be exalted” (Luke Job 14:11).
13: Certainly God does not hear a hollow cry; the Almighty does not regard it. The authentic cry that God does hear: Heb Job 5:7 “in the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence.” Christ’s cry is the most genuine cry ever made — and Ps 22:24 confirms God heard it: “he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one.”
14: How much less when you say you cannot see him, that the case is before him, and you must wait for him. The counsel to wait in trust. Heb Job 6:15: “Abraham, having patiently waited, obtained the promise.” The waiting and trusting that Elihu recommends is fulfilled in Christ who “endured the cross, despising the shame, and is seated at the right hand of the throne of God” (Heb Job 12:2).
15: But now, because God has not punished in his anger and does not take sharp notice of every transgression — The patience of God before judgment: 2 Pet Job 3:9: “the Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance.” The delay of punishment is grace extended; it finds its ultimate expression in the cross where sin is dealt with once for all.
16: “So Job opens his mouth with empty words; he heaps up words without knowledge.” The final judgment on Job by Elihu — which God himself overturns in ch. 42 (“Job has spoken what is right”). Christ is the one whose words are never empty: John Job 6:63 “the words that I have spoken to you are spirit and life.” He is the Word without emptiness, knowledge without limit.
